chapters.title: How Steve Eisman entered Wall Street through family ties
chapters.text:
Raised in New York City and trained at Harvard Law School, Steve Eisman eventually left corporate law for finance. His parents, Lillian and Elliot, were accomplished brokers at Oppenheimer during the early 1960s, advising entrepreneurs and investors when performance alone determined whether analysts earned their keep. When their thirty-year-old son grew restless in law, they brought him into the firm as a junior equity analyst.

At first his role carried little weight. Oppenheimer's nepotism policy required his parents to cover his salary during the first year while the firm decided whether to keep him. Eisman proved capable and survived probation.

Children often mirror parents who appear fulfilled and financially secure in their careers.

Within a year Eisman rose from overlooked assistant to lead analyst covering Aames Financial, a fledgling subprime mortgage lender. He won the assignment by speaking confidently about work he had done on a comparable deal as a lawyer, then learned the business on the fly. Lomas Financial Corp. followed Aames on his roster.

Over time Eisman became an unusual Wall Street personality—more driven by his own convictions than by outside opinion. He cared less about consensus and more about whether the numbers in his head made sense. If you want to understand how mortgage lending, interest rates, and subprime bonds work—and how traders like Eisman profited by betting against that market—this summary is a strong starting point.

chapters.title: Mortgage bonds reshaped credit—and Eisman struggled to raise capital
chapters.text:
When Eisman arrived in finance, mortgage bonds were gaining traction. They extended credit to buyers who otherwise could not afford homes, letting them purchase property or extract equity from houses they already owned.

Packaging home loans into bonds also solved an earlier investor headache: borrowers refinancing whenever rates fell, repaying early and leaving bondholders sitting on cash they did not want.

As mortgage loans became popular and only a handful of people jumped at them, investors started developing cold feet about putting their money forth. The reason was that mortgage borrowers became clever, repaying their loans only when the interest rates were low to refinance cheaply, usually leaving investors with piles of cash when they least needed it.

Salomon Brothers addressed that risk by slicing homeowner payments into tranches, each carrying different interest levels.

In early 2004 Morgan Stanley recruited Eisman to launch a fund focused on financial firms—banks, Wall Street houses, and mortgage companies. Capital was scarce. Financial strain mounted until his wife warned they might abandon finance entirely if the effort failed.

Eisman pressed on, touring Europe and the United States to find backers. An insurer eventually committed fifty million dollars, and colleagues such as Vincent Daniel, Olympic rower Porter Collins, and trader Danny Moses joined the effort.

Did you know? Steve Eisman and his wife Valerie nearly opened a bed-and-breakfast when fundraising for his fund proved so difficult.

chapters.title: Eisman and Michael Burry bet against subprime—and won big
chapters.text:
By early 2005 Eisman's team operated like a subprime research engine, planning to short the market. Short selling means borrowing a security, selling it, then repurchasing after the price falls and pocketing the spread.

Subprime lending had exploded since the mid-1990s, with loans increasingly repackaged as bonds.

Even as lending terms shifted, the share of floating-rate subprime loans climbed—from roughly two-thirds fixed-rate in 1996 to about eighty percent adjustable after an initial fixed period by the mid-2000s.

The subprime loan market taught the lesson: don't make loans to people who can't repay them; instead, make loans, then sell them to the fixed income departments of investment banks, which will, in turn, package them into bonds and sell them to investors. ~ Michael Lewis

Every major Wall Street bank had entered mortgage origination by 2005. Eisman suspected portions of the housing market would implode and began structuring shorts. Goldman Sachs later faced fierce criticism for allegedly misleading investors while profiting from the collapse.

Eisman and physician-turned-investor Michael Burry became synonymous with wagering against toxic mortgage debt. Burry placed his first bond-market bet in 2004. After studying American lending patterns, he taught himself how subprime loans were structured and invented credit default swaps—a new instrument that let him short housing by betting prices would fall.

Clients panicked when banks insisted the market was stabilizing and home values kept rising. Burry held his positions and ultimately delivered roughly five-hundred-percent returns to those who stayed.

chapters.title: A wave of investors joined the bet against mortgage bonds
chapters.text:
Eisman, like Burry, used credit default swaps to harvest enormous gains—but they were hardly alone. In 2006 analyst Greg Lippmann, who had reached similar conclusions, met privately with investors to explain the trade. A PerTrac study later found nearly fourteen thousand hedge funds involved. Institutional players could also buy swaps on subprime bonds, and many traded relative value by purchasing one bond while shorting another.

Writing a check separates a commitment from a conversation. ~ Warren Buffett

Some participants wagered directly against the entire subprime complex and, by extension, the global financial system. Hedge fund manager John Paulson, tipped off by another analyst, accumulated billions in swaps on mortgage bonds.

Traders who shorted subprime became consumed by the gamble. Figures like Eisman had only recently grasped the system's fragility, then positioned themselves against it. Burry, Paulson, and others all managed to get in.

Paulson scaled the swap trade larger than most because he raised massive sums from outside investors.

Charlie Ledley pursued a different angle, targeting outcomes others deemed nearly impossible. He and his partners made that strategy work repeatedly. Partnering with Jamie Mai, he hunted long-shot bets with outsized payoffs. Trader Ben Hockett later supplied deep fixed-income expertise, helping the pair unload swaps as prices collapsed and netting more than eighty million dollars.

Did you know? A hedge fund pools capital—usually from wealthy investors—to pursue aggressive strategies with lighter regulation than mutual funds, allowing riskier positions.

chapters.title: The subprime market unraveled in 2007
chapters.text:
In 2007 Greg Lippmann saw U.S. home prices sliding and subprime defaults climbing, then shorted ten billion dollars of mortgage bonds.

That year marked the start of America's subprime crisis, a 2007–2010 shock that fed the global meltdown, triggered widespread delinquencies, and erased trillions in housing wealth.

The preceding bubble rested on mortgage-backed securities and collateralized debt obligations that promised higher yields, attractive ratings, and better returns than government debt. Warning signs surfaced in 2007; by September 2008 several iconic institutions had failed.

Two drivers stood out: surging subprime issuance—jumping from seven percent or less to roughly twenty percent between 2004 and 2006—and speculative buying, with investor share of originations rising sharply into 2007. Many 2006 subprime loans carried rates that reset upward over time.

The 2008–2010 global financial crisis left countless mortgage investors ruined and displaced, especially across the United States, as delinquencies spread and home values cratered nationwide.

Between 2005 and 2007 Lippmann built a five-billion-dollar short. In 2008, at senior management's direction, his desk booked more than 1.2 billion dollars on a single position—reportedly Deutsche Bank's most profitable trade ever—then left to start his own fund.

Burry, another early winner, faced bizarre rumors after his success: some claimed he abandoned his wife and hid in South America. He wrote publicly to deny the gossip and reaffirm his commitment to markets.

chapters.title: The shorts cashed in—and wrestled with what it meant
chapters.text:
Lippmann pictured subprime's endgame: Wall Street churning out loans, bonds, and CDOs while originators fabricated credit on the other side. These discoveries spurred him to go on with his bet.

Eisman likewise understood that selling securities you do not own could mint easy money—yet recognized the moral weight. By 2008 the entire financial system teetered. Still, in late 2007 FrontPoint, his firm, doubled its fund size with anti-subprime bets.

Vincent Daniel served as Eisman's lead researcher at FrontPoint Partners. Despite coming from a humbler background, he investigated subprime lending to validate Eisman's thesis and grew wealthy when the market cracked.

Danny Moses, Eisman's head trader, admired Eisman's stubbornness and market ambition. The team's optimist, Moses gathered intelligence throughout the boom. He and Daniel prospered from the shorts yet later felt uneasy about making money this way, troubled that their gains rode on other people's losses.

Colleagues who joined Eisman's wager often treated his positions as personal insults to Wall Street orthodoxy.

When critics blamed financially irresponsible homeowners, Eisman countered that lenders encouraged borrowers to falsify applications. He reserved his anger for executives atop the lending machine, not applicants at the bottom.

Eisman had publicly clashed with many of Wall Street's most prominent names.

Because the subprime gamble depended on continued defaults, the group leaned heavily on credit default swaps and waited for loans to fail.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The Big Short chronicles the financiers who foresaw—and profited from—the housing market's 2006 collapse.

As this summary shows, subprime lending destabilized the mortgage system. Borrowers who could not truly afford their payments still signed loans, creating extreme default risk. Lenders offloaded that risk to investment banks, which securitized it into mortgage-backed securities and collateralized debt obligations.

Investing and gambling overlap uncomfortably here. Analysts who won—including lawyer Steve Eisman, whose parents ushered him into finance; the abrasive Greg Lippmann; the reclusive Michael Burry; and Eisman's lieutenants Moses and Daniel—bet heavily on their forecasts. Short sellers at firms like Cornwall Capital collected millions.

Most Wall Street CEOs lost fortunes yet escaped bankruptcy through government rescue. Those who financed the risk-taking were ignorant of its consequences; they were only after the profits from people who made complicated bets.

The men on the trading floor may not have been to school, but they have Ph.D.s in man's ignorance. ~ Michael Lewis

Try this:

If this overview sparked your curiosity, watch the 2015 film adaptation—about 130 minutes blending education and drama. The movie follows Eisman, Burry, and others as they uncover the rot inside mortgage-backed securities.

Read it till the end?
